measures_statement: YES — the statement says that all or some measures are available
measures_statement_verbatim:
1. Participants were asked whether they had ever experienced gender euphoria, with the explanation that gender euphoria refers to “an experience of feeling totally affirmed in one’s gender” (Benestad, Citation2010). Response options included “NO,” “Yes, I am currently experiencing gender euphoria,” “Yes, in the past 12 months,” and “Yes, more than 12 months ago.”

2. Feeling a part of the LGBTQ community was assessed by asking participant the extent to which they agreed with the statement “You feel you’re part of the Australian LGBTIQ community.” Responses were recorded on a 5-point Likert scale ranging from strongly agree to strongly disagree. For the purpose of the analyses, responses were dichotomized into positive (“strongly agree” and “agree”) and not positive (“neither agree nor disagree,” “disagree” and “strongly disagree”). 

3. Being able to access gender affirming care was assessed by asking participants to respond to the item “I have been easily able to access gender affirming care when I have needed to” with responses ranging on a 5-point Likert scale from “Strongly disagree” to “Strongly agree” or the option to choose “NOt applicable.” In order to meaningfully interpret the outcomes, responses were dichotomized into positive (“strongly agree” and “agree”) and not positive (“neither agree nor disagree,” “disagree” and “strongly disagree”).

4. Recent experiences of suicidal ideation and suicide attempt were assessed by asking participants if they had experienced any thoughts related to suicide or wanting to end their life as well as if they had attempted suicide or tried to end their life. Response options to these items included “NO,” “Yes, in the past 12 months,” “Yes, more than 12 months ago,” and “Prefer not to answer.” Participants were allowed to select multiple responses if they did not select “NO” or “Prefer not to answer.

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. The ten-item dissociation subscale of the Trauma Symptom Inventory-2 (Briere, Citation2011) was used to measure dissociative experiences. Items include “feeling like you were in a dream,” “feeling like you were outside your body,” “feeling like things weren’t real,” “spacing out,” “finding yourself some place and not knowing how you got there,” “people saying you don’t pay enough attention to what’s going on around you,” “feeling like there were two or more people inside you,” “not feeling like your real self,” “having trouble remembering the details about something bad that happened to you,” and “feeling like you were watching yourself from far away.” Respondents were asked to report how frequently they had each experience in the last six months using a four-point Likert scale including response options Never (0), Rarely (1), Sometimes (2), and Often (3)

2. A modified version of the seven-item Adolescent Psychotic-like Symptom Screener (APSS: Kelleher et al., Citation2011) was used. Items include “Some people believe that their thoughts can be read by another person. Have other people ever read your mind?” and “Have you ever felt you were under the control of some special power?” Participants first indicated how often they had each experience on a four-point Likert scale (Never, Sometimes, Often, and Nearly Always), and then how distressed they were by each experience, also on a four-point Likert scale (NOt distressed, A bit distressed, Quite distressed, and Very distressed). A “symptom” of psychosis was present if (a) the frequency was rated as Sometimes or above, and (b) the distress was rated as A bit distressed or more. 


3. Participants were asked to indicate how many times in the last 12 months they had visited their general practitioner (G.P.), how many times in the last 12 months they had visited a mental health professional such as a psychiatrist, clinical psychologist, or therapist, how many different medications they had been prescribed in the last 12 months, and how many times they had visited a hospital for treatment in the last 12 months.

1. Sex work was assessed using the question, “Have you ever had sex with someone who paid you to do so?” A dichotomous yes or no response option was available.

2. Educational mobility is a measure of the difference a respondent’s educational attainment compared to their parent’s educational attainment. Using self-reports, the highest level of educational attainment from either parent (measured at Wave I) and the highest level of educational attainment of a respondent (measured at Wave V) were classified into the following categories: (1) less than high school, (2) high school graduate, (3) some college/vocational school, (4) college graduate, and (5) postgraduate.

coi_statement_verbatim: With DQA studies, it is essential that researchers practice reflexivity—acknowledging their influence on the research—and engage in rigorous analysis; otherwise, there is the risk that confirmation bias will distort results (Gilgun, Citation2014). All authors are male, White, and clinically trained. The first author is a graduate student and the second and third authors are professors. All authors have published previously on the process of healing from infidelity. More specifically, the authors of this paper are the developers of the hypothesized model, a practice-based model developed over years of treating couples who have experienced infidelity. We recognized the limitations of our model, given its grounding in our clinical experience and its lack of empirical scrutiny, and we desired to begin the process of improving it through systematic evaluation.

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. Round 1 of the modified Delphi took place in January 2023 and included completion of an initial asynchronous survey via Qualtrics in which participants (N = 25) rated all items derived from the qualitative interviews. Participants identified whether items for each aim should be included using a rating scale ranging from 1 (definitely no) to 9 (definitely yes) with the midpoint rating of a 5 representing a response of uncertain/don’t know. Question A asked “Should [item] inform if, when and how MBSR is covered by health insurance?” Question B asked “Should [item] be included in the systematic review protocol?” Question C was divided into two parts, which asked “Is [item] a barrier / something that makes it harder for MBSR to be covered by health insurance (e.g., with its own health insurance billing code)?” and “Is [item] a facilitator / something that makes it easier for MBSR to be covered by health insurance (e.g., with its own health insurance billing code)?” In addition to rating the importance of each item, participants also completed a demographics form and rated their knowledge of mindfulness.

measures_statement_verbatim: The pre- and post-lockdown consumption questions were adapted from a previous survey examining dietary changes during COVID-19 lockdowns [27]. Pre-lockdown consumption frequency was assessed by asking “BEFORE the lockdown, how often did you eat the following (portions of) foods? Please indicate how often you consumed at least one portion of the following foods and drinks” (1 = Never/Almost Never to 7 = Two or more times per day) for the four dietary outcomes of alcohol, sweet snacks, salty snacks, and sugary beverages. The use of a frequency measure permitted equivalent response options to be applied across the product categories. The next question asked “DURING the lockdown, how often did you eat the following (portions of) foods? Please indicate how often you consumed at least one portion of the following foods and drinks” (1 = Never/Almost Never to 7 = Two or more times per day), listing the same product categories as the previous question.

measures_statement_verbatim:
NOTE: no availability statement for 2nd measure

Using survey items based on previous behavioral epidemiologic studies of adolescent cannabis use (Han et al., 2024; Leventhal et al., 2020, Peters et al., 2018), baseline self-reported past 6-month cannabis use for fun or to get high was measured using separate items for each cannabis product type: (1) smoking marijuana, (2) electronic device to vape THC or hash oil, (3) THC/marijuana edibles, foods, or drinks (hereafter “edibles”), and (4) CBD or hemp products. For each of the four questions, we analyzed the raw response options (yes/no) as binary exposure variables. We additionally created a binary exposure variable separating past 6-month use of any cannabis product (smoking, vaping, edibles, or CBD/hemp products) vs. no past 6-month use of these products. We also created a past 6-month poly-cannabis use variable (count exposure [range: 0–4] and categorical exposure [response options: 0, 1, 2 + ]).

Secondary measures of exposure used in supplemental analyses were based on parallel questions for past 30-day use for each cannabis product. Additionally, a subset of those reporting use of CBD or hemp products received follow-up questions assessing the type of cannabinoids that were in the CBD or hemp products used in past-6-months (check all that apply: CBD only, CBD+THC blend, Delta-9-THC, or other cannabinoid [Delta-8, Delta-10, HHC, CBG, CBN]) which we included for descriptive purposes.

measures_statement_verbatim:
"Any correctional policy documents explicitly focused upon the care and management of trans people, along with any correctional policy documents referenced within the trans care policies, were reviewed once to identify their focuses when it came to the carceral management of trans people. Following this initial appraisal, they were reviewed  once more and tabulated along the following thematic areas:


1. Provisions for the classification of a person as trans in official carceral documentation, including whether diverse identities such as non-binary persons as recognized within the policy documents.
2. Provisions for placement decisions.
3. Provisions for the use of solitary confinement and/or administrative segregation.
4. Provisions for access to transition-related
healthcare.
5. Provisions for intimate searches.
6. Provisions for access to gender-affirming clothing and belongings."

measures_statement_verbatim: "The questionnaire included 94 items addressing compliance with 26 clinical SSI preventive interventions, COM determinants, socio-demographics, and awareness of in-house implementation strategies (not reported here). The COM determinants were surveyed using 18 items on a 7-point-Likert-scale (“strongly disagree“ to “strongly agree”). In accordance with the previous exploratory factor analysis (EFA),15 the number of items measuring ‘capability’, “opportunity”, “motivation”, and “planning” were six, six, four, and two, respectively (for items, see Table S1). The SSI preventive intervention measures for which self-reported compliance was assessed were selected in accordance with national recommendations (see Table S2). For each of the interventions, participants were asked to indicate in what percentage of the opportunities in which an intervention is indicated they actually performed the intervention compliantly. Participants could select ”not applicable” if they felt an intervention was outside their area of responsibility. The overall compliance rate for all interventions combined was calculated as in:15 summing the compliance rate for each intervention divided by the number of interventions for which responsibility was indicated."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "4.2. Sample size, treatment condition, comparison arms
Treatment and comparison arm information was taken from the one-line study summary. Terms drawn directly from the paper were distilled down to groupings of treatment conditions and comparison arms. A full list of treatment conditions and comparison arms can be found in Appendix Tables A1 and A2.
4.3. Population and outcome measures
Population and outcome measures for each study were extracted using a combination of information from the summary field and the study title. Because RCTs often study the intersection of different groups, we decided upon a hierarchy of populations so that we could best characterize the “primary” population of each study in a variegated way. The hierarchy used was as follows.

1. Medical conditions
2. Studies of parents or caregivers
3. Studies of couples
4. Studies of behavioral health issues (smoking, substance use, diet, etc.)
5. Mental disorders
6. All other issues
7. If none of the above was present, the study population was coded as the demographic category that the participants belong to (i.e., students, older adults, etc.)

The rationale for this ordering is that while the population categories higher up on the hierarchy are often comorbid with DSM-defined mental disorders, studies of these populations are qualitatively distinct in that they tend to look at different outcomes than those seen in studies focusing solely on mental disorders. For instance, studies of medical conditions often look at physical functioning and quality of life. Studies of parents or caregivers typically look at coping and burnout, studies of couples look at relationship quality, and studies of behavioral health issues look at behavioral outcomes. Therefore, it is useful to distinguish these studies from those of mental disorders more generally.
As an example, if an RCT listed their population as “smokers with depression,” the primary population is coded as “smokers.” Likewise, if the population was listed as “individuals with HIV and comorbid anxiety,” the primary population would be coded as “HIV.” A study would only be coded with a population of a demographic category such as “older adults” if there are no other inclusion criteria higher in the hierarchy. For instance, “Older adults with depression” would be coded as “depression.” Due to the large number of studies whose populations had comorbid mental disorders, the primary study population does not fully reflect whether studies focused on mental disorders. Therefore, to provide an additional estimate of the total number of studies related to DSM-defined disorders, we performed a supplementary analysis that began with these indicators as will be described below.

Up to 3 possible outcome measures were allowed for each study. If more than 3 primary outcomes were listed in the title or summary of the study, only the first 3 mentioned in the title, abstract, or text were listed. Outcomes were combined if they were functionally equivalent. For example, “emotional status,” “emotional wellbeing,” “psychological wellbeing,” and “wellbeing” were all simply coded as “wellbeing.” In situations where a specific mental disorder was mentioned in the study summary or title, it was assumed that symptoms or features of that disorder were captured as an outcome. For example, if the study title or summary mentioned “social anxiety,” one of the outcomes would be coded as “social anxiety symptoms” even if it was not explicitly listed in the abstract as an outcome.

The above processes initially resulted in 119 unique populations and 413 unique outcomes. These were grouped based on similarity into 10 more general population categories and 40 more general outcome categories for analysis. A list of the final population and outcome categories, as well as examples of the original unique categories that make them up, can be found in Appendix Tables A3 and A4. Additionally, the full crosswalk of original to final categories can be found along with our full dataset on OSF (Hayes & King, 2024)."


2. "4.3.1. Alternative characterization of studies of DSM disorders
Given the fact that our method of coding the primary population of each study gives precedence to some other categories (medical illness/physical impairment, parents/caregivers, couples concerns, behavioral health) over DSM diagnoses, the primary study population does not fully capture studies of DSM-defined disorders that co-occur with the above categories. We therefore performed two additional analyses to answer the question “Is this a study of ACT for treatment of a DSM-defined disorder?” in the absence of any other population information. We will label these analyses as our “conservative characterization” and “liberal characterization” of DSM-disorder-related studies, defined as follows.
•
Conservative characterization: a study is coded as DSM-disorder-related if the inclusion criteria for the study include a DSM diagnosis or a cutoff on a scale or test that measures symptoms of a DSM diagnosis – for instance, a study would be categorized this way if participants were those with a depression score of 10 or higher on the Depression Anxiety Stress Scale (DASS; Lovibond & Lovibond, 1995).
•
Liberal characterization: a study is coded as DSM-disorder-related if it either meets the criteria for the conservative characterization, or additionally, if a scale or test that measures symptoms of a DSM diagnosis (such as the DASS) is among the primary outcome measures of the study."


3. "4.4. Journal statistics
Journal names were extracted from the study citation. Journal data were collected by first searching for the journal name on the ISSN portal (ISSN International Center, nd) and SCImago Journal & Country Rank (SCImago, nd) to find the ISSN and country of publication. For journals that did not appear on these websites, the journal title was searched on Google to find the journal website, which listed the ISSN and country of publication in the vast majority of cases. ISSN and journal metrics were left blank for journals if no website listing their ISSN could be found.
After finding the journal ISSN, it was entered into the Resurchify Journal Ranking (Resurchify, nd) to collect the impact score, H-Index, and SJR. The 2022 versions of each statistic were used, as they were the most recent available at the time of data collection. Journals that did not have available metrics through Resurchify were labeled as “non-indexed” journals. The country that a study is associated with was determined by the publication location of the journal. It should be noted that author location information is at times unavailable or incomplete, especially in LMIC studies, and authors sometimes come from multiple different countries. Information on the location of study populations is also frequently missing. For these reasons, using the journal publication location was the most practical way to geographically describe a study. This information was sorted into either lower- and middle-income countries (LMIC) or high-income countries (HIC) based on the FY24 World Bank Group country classifications by income level (World Bank, 2023). Low, lower-middle, and upper-middle income groups were included within our LMIC label.
While it was not possible to categorize studies based on the country of origin of authors or the study populations, even a cursory exploration of the ACBS list shows that journals published in LMIC largely serve authors from LMIC regions, publishing studies conducted with local populations. In many of these journals, articles were published in languages other than English."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. Anthropometric measures included height, weight, BMI,
body fat percentage, waist circumference, hip circumference, blood pressure, and heart rate. Weight and body
composition (body fat percentage) were determined using a
standard bioelectrical impedance device (BF 511, OMRON
Healthcare, Mannheim, Germany). Waist circumference
was measured with a measuring tape positioned in the horizontal plane exactly midway between the iliac crest and
the costal arch, using the mean score of two consecutive
measurements [78]. Hip circumference was measured in the
horizontal plain at the maximal circumference of the hips or
buttock region above the gluteal fold, and two consecutive
measurements were averaged. Blood pressure and heart rate were examined using a standard automated digital blood
pressure monitor on the side with the higher blood pressure values, using the arithmetic mean of two consecutive
measurements for analyses.

2. Blood serum markers included triglycerides, cholesterol,
HDL and LDL cholesterol, fasting glucose, AST (aspartate aminotransferase), ALT (alanine aminotransferase) and
GGT (gamma-glutamyltransferase), uric acid, and creatinine. All participants were asked to present fasted for the
blood drawing; i.e., the appointments were scheduled in
the early mornings (before Ramadan), and in the evenings
(within the last 2 days of Ramadan, before fast breaking
and festivities). All haemal markers were determined by a
certifed and DIN EN ISO 15189:2014 accredited hospital
laboratory in Essen, Germany

measures_statement: YES — the statement says that all or some measures are available
measures_statement_verbatim:
1. Delay Discounting Task - study 1
Participants completed two hypothetical monetary delay discounting tasks: one in which delays were presented as typical delays, and one in which delays were presented as dates (DeHart & Odum, 2015). The order of task presentation was randomized and counterbalanced across both the gains and the losses conditions, respectively. Therefore, roughly half of participants experienced the date-framed discounting task prior to the delay-framed discounting task, and roughly half of participants completed the delay-framed discounting task followed by the date-framed discounting task. Prior to completing the tasks, participants read the following instructions (cf. Odum et al., 2006):

The following choices are hypothetical, and you will not receive the actual outcomes. There are no “right” or “wrong” answers. Please just pick the choice that you prefer. Please note that the choices will switch sides randomly across questions. Please give special attention to the units of time as well as the amount that you are being asked about.

Participants then made choices between immediate or delayed hypothetical monetary gains or losses for each trial. For the monetary gains condition, participants were asked to make decisions such as “Would you prefer $50 now or $100 in one month?” In contrast, for the monetary losses condition, participants were asked “Would you prefer to pay $50 now or $100 in one month?”


2. Delay Discounting Tasks - study 2
Participants completed two delay discounting tasks, one with a smaller delayed magnitude and one with a larger delayed magnitude (i.e., $10 and $100). At the start of the survey, the order of the delay discounting tasks was randomly assigned and counterbalanced across participants. Therefore, about half of participants (n = 34) completed the small magnitude task before completing the large magnitude task (small-large group) and about half of the participants (n = 37) completed the large magnitude followed by the small (large-small group). Participants chose between an immediate amount of hypothetical money or a delayed amount of hypothetical money on each trial. The immediate amounts changed across trials and were presented in descending order (cf. Odum et al., 2006). The small magnitude immediate amounts were $10, $9.50, $9, $8.50, $8, $7.50, $7, $6.50, $6, $5.50, $5, $4.50, $4, $3.50, $3, $2.50, $1.50, $1, $0.50, $0.10; and the immediate amounts for the large magnitude task were 10 times the amount of the small magnitude (i.e., $100, $95, $90, etc.). Choices were made across four delays (1 week, 1 month, 1 year, and 25 years) that were presented in ascending order (Odum et al., 2006). Indifference points were calculated as the last immediate amount chosen at each delay, as in our previous study that employed adjusting-amount (as in Experiment 1) and fixed-sequence (as in Experiment 2) procedures (Rodzon et al., 2011). For participants who always chose the larger option at a particular delay, we used the amount of the larger option as the indifference point. Before completing the tasks, participants were given the following instructions (cf. Odum et al., 2006):

The following questions will ask you to make some hypothetical decisions about which of two rewards you prefer. You will not receive the rewards that you choose but make your decisions as though you were really going to receive these rewards you choose. You are going to be asked to choose between an immediate amount delivered now versus a delayed amount that you would get after waiting the delay interval. Click the reward that you would prefer. The choices you make are completely up to you. Please select the option that you prefer, not what you think I want you to prefer. It is not expected that you will choose one particular reward over the other. Just choose the one you really want.

Following instructions, hypothetical amounts and delays were presented on the screen, and participants clicked on the amount they preferred. The following notes appeared on the screen when the delay or amount changed: (1) “Please note that the amount of time to the delayed reward has changed for the following questions” (2) “Please note that the amount of money and the amount of time to the delayed reward has changed for the following questions.”

measures_statement_verbatim: The primary outcome variables were self-reported depression (i.e., feeling sad or hopeless), anxiety, and using alcohol and drugs as a coping strategy. All items measuring mental health concerns were obtained from the Youth Risk Behavior Survey (Eaton et al., Citation2012). These items were not used to diagnose but rather to give a broad indication of whether the youth participant was experiencing or had experienced these types of mental health concerns. We measured self-reported depression using the item, “In the past year, did you ever feel so sad or hopeless almost every day for two weeks or more in a row that you stopped doing your usual activities?” Youth could respond, “yes” or “no.” Anxiety was measured using the item, “In the past month, how often have you been so worried about something that you could not sleep at night?” Youth could answer “never,” “sometimes,” or “often.” Next, a binary variable for anxiety at night was made by collapsing the “sometimes” and “always” response options to create the dichotomous response options “yes” or “no.” Finally, we assessed substance use as a coping strategy using the item, “In the past month how often have you been so worried about something that you wanted to use drugs or alcohol to feel better?” Youth could answer “never,” “sometimes,” or “often.” A binary variable for using alcohol and drugs as a coping strategy for anxiety was made by collapsing the “sometimes” and “often” response options to create the dichotomous response options “yes” or “no.”

measures_statement_verbatim:
Sexual behaviour measures
Frequency of behavior
The frequency of pornography use, masturbation, and sexual intercourse was assessed with a single item with five options, ranging from “Less than once a month” to “More than once a day” (see Supplement A for all items and response options of all questionnaires).

Recency of behavior
The recency of pornography use, masturbation, and sexual intercourse was assessed with a single item with five options, ranging from “More than a month ago/Never” to “Today.”

Motivation to quit
Motivation to quit pornography use was measured using an adapted version of the contemplation ladder (Biener & Abrams, Citation1991; Hogue, Dauber, & Morgenstern, Citation2010) which had 10 response options and four anchors: “1) I have no interest in reducing my porn use at all; 4) I am somewhat motivated to reduce my porn use; 7) I am very motivated to reduce my porn use; 10) I am fully motivated to completely quit using porn.”

Compulsive masturbation with pornography
This was assessed with an adapted version of the Compulsivity subscale from the Attitudes Toward Masturbation Scale (Young & Muehlenhard, Citation2019), substituting the original “masturbation” with “pornography.” This is a five-item measure with a seven-point response scale ranging from “NOt a reason” to “Very important reason.” Internal consistency as assessed with Cronbach’s Alpha was satisfactory (α = 0.825).

Problematic pornography use
PPU was assessed with the Problematic Pornography Consumption Scale (PPCS; Bőthe et al., Citation2018) which is an 18-item instrument inquiring about PPU in the past six months. It has a seven-point response scale ranging from “Never” to “All the time.” Internal consistency as assessed with Cronbach’s Alpha was satisfactory (α = 0.933).

State measures of desire for pornography use and restraint self-efficacy
These constructs were used to assess craving in the study and they were measured with two items per construct. Desire for pornography use was assessed with the items “I want to watch pornography and masturbate right now” and “My desire to view pornography and masturbate feels overpowering right now.” Self-efficacy related to pornography use was assessed with the items “At this moment, I feel I can control my urges to view pornography and masturbate” and “Right now, I could stop or distract myself from thoughts related to pornography and masturbation.” The response scale for all items was a seven-point scale ranging from “NOt at all” to “Very much.” These two constructs were measured at four points during the study, with acceptable levels of internal consistency for both the desire measure (αs between 0.690 and 0.788) and self-efficacy measure (αs between 0.705 and 0.839).

Individual difference measures
Life meaning
This was assessed with the Multidimensional Existential Meaning Scale (MEMS; George & Park, Citation2016b), which is a 15-item instrument inquiring about the three aspects of life meaning (comprehension, purpose, mattering). It has a seven-point response scale ranging from “Strongly disagree” to “Strongly agree.” Internal consistency as assessed with Cronbach’s Alpha was satisfactory (α = 0.917).

Boredom susceptibility
This was assessed with the Boredom Proneness Scale-Short Form (BPS-SR; Struk, Carriere, Cheyne, & Danckert, Citation2017), which is an eight-item instrument with a five-point response scale ranging from “Strongly disagree” to “Strongly agree.” Internal consistency as assessed with Cronbach’s Alpha was satisfactory (α = 0.867).

Depressive symptoms
Depression was assessed using an adapted version (reduced response scale from seven to four) of the Major Depression Inventory from ICD-10 (MDI; Olsen, Jensen, NOerholm, Martiny, & Bech, Citation2003), which is a 12-item instrument with a response scale ranging from “NOt at all” to “Nearly every day.” Internal consistency as assessed with Cronbach’s Alpha was satisfactory (α = 0.810).

Manipulation check
After the interventions, participants completed six items to assess whether the interventions had their intended effects. The items used a ten-point response scale ranging from “NOne” to “Completely” to assess the following: “How much of the following states do you experience, at this moment?” followed by the six descriptors of “a sense of meaning”; “willpower and determination”; “awareness and mindfulness”; “boredom”; “awe and wonder”; and “part of something greater than myself” (a descriptor used to assess connectedness).

Cue exposure
Before the interventions, there was a five-minute pornography cue exposure section which was designed to activate craving for pornography use. The instructions before the cue exposure repeated (information from informed consent) that the participants would be asked to browse online and watch pornographic content of their choice for five minutes. Participants were asked to refrain from masturbation during the session. There was a visible timer on the subsequent page of the study, with detailed instructions: “1. You can see the timer, which indicates how much time you have left for viewing pornography. In order to keep track of the timer, do not close this window; 2. When the timer expires, the red button will reappear which will allow you to continue the study; 3. When you access pornography, keep watching it until the timer expires. Keep track of the time; 4. You can now open a new tab or window in your browser and search for pornographic content.” When the timer expired, participants could proceed to the intervention section.

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "Alcohol use. At Time 1 (T1) and Time 2 (T2), participants reported on their alcohol use via three questions on the Alcohol, Tobacco, and Other Drugs (ATOD) questionnaire (Elliott et al., 1985). Youths indicated how many times in the past 3 months they used or tried beer, wine or wine coolers, and hard liquor, using a 5-point scale (1 “never”; 2 “less than once per week”; 3 “about once per week”; 4 “two or three times per week”; 5 “almost every day or every day”). Frequency of alcohol use was computed by taking the mean of the three items, coefficient alpha (α) = 0.78 and 0.72 at T1 and T2, respectively."


2. "baseline ECG was recorded while seated for 3 min, using a Biopac fMRI compatible wireless system recording at 400 Hz (Biopac Systems, USA) via Siemens’ telnet MPCU. The data were transformed into an ASCII-formatted string of amplitude values, and then input into the Mindware HRV program (Mindware Technologies, Gahanna, OH). Inter-beat-interval sequence was used to compute RSA. Inter-beat intervals were calculated using the delay time between consecutive local maxima in the QRS complex (R-spikes) (Berntson et al., 1997). Research assistants were trained to visually review ECG data for accurate identification of R-spikes in order to manually correct the data in instances when the automated program misinterpreted the R-spikes. The age appropriate respiratory frequency band setting, 0.12 − 0.40, was used to measure RSA (Dollar et al., 2020). Mindware calculates RSA as the natural log of spectral power in this frequency range. Baseline RSA was calculated in 30-s epochs, and then averaged across the 3-min baseline period."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. Vicarious discrimination was assessed in Wave 3 using an adapted
version of the Vicarious Discrimination Scale (Chae et al., 2021). Our
adapted version contained three questions (Hahm, Ha, et al., 2021). In
the prompt, participants were asked to “think about the experiences of
Asian or Asian American people.” Using a seven-item Likert scale that
ranged from never to almost every day, participants were asked “Since
the COVID-19 pandemic, how often did you …” and presented with
the three items, “[h]ear about or see racist things about Asian/Asian
American people posted on the news, social media, or online?,”
“[w]itness Asian/Asian American people in person or in virtual meetings being treated unfairly because of their race (not incidents you hear
about or see online)?,” “[h]ear about or witness Asian/Asian American
friends, family, or loved ones experience racism?” The sum score for
vicarious discrimination was used in our analysis.

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. At the start of each of the three laboratory sessions, the participants
provided an exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) sample, submitted a
urine sample for cotinine and anabasine measurement (Jacob et al.,
2002), and completed a staff-collected timeline follow-back
assessment (Brown et al., 1998) to document their daily cigarette
consumption (cigarettes per day [CPD]) since their previous session.
Additionally, we assessed 7-day point prevalence abstinence,
biochemically verified using a CO level of less than 5 ppm as the
threshold, at 8 weeks posttraining.

2. To determine the effects of ABM training on behavioral measures
of AB, the participants completed the dot-probe and Stroop AB
tasks following the smoking assessments at each laboratory session.
The dot-probe AB task was comprised of 160 trials (pairs of 16
unique smoking and neutral pictures, pseudorandomly repeated
10 times in four blocks), separated by a fixation cross. Each picture
pair was probed after a 200-ms presentation time, with probes
pseudorandomly appearing after each picture an equal number of
times (Bradley et al., 2003). The participants were instructed to press
one of two keyboard keys as swiftly as possible to indicate the side
of the screen where the probe emerged. A dot-probe AB score wascomputed by calculating the average difference in reaction time
(RT) between the smoking and neutral cue targets on trials with
accurate responses, with more positive scores indicating a greater
AB toward smoking cues (Attwood et al., 2008; Mueller et al.,
2009). As we previously reported (J. D. Robinson et al., 2022), the
split-half internal reliability for the dot-probe AB task was small in
strength of association (r = 0.29).

3. The participants completed the eye-tracking AB assessment
following the laboratory behavioral AB assessments. The eyetracking AB task involved a paired-picture presentation, similar to
the ABM training modified dot-probe tasks but with a longer
presentation (1,500 ms vs. 200 ms) and no dot-probes. The primary
eye-tracking measure was the mean proportion of gaze dwell time
spent looking at the smoking relative to the neutral cues at each
session, which was calculated by dividing the total dwell time spent
looking at the smoking cues by the sum of the total dwell time spent
looking at the smoking and neutral cues. The laboratory eyetracking AB dot-probe task consisted of 160 trials.
Eye-tracking data were collected using a Tobii TX300 (Tobii AB,
Stockholm, Sweden) with a 300-Hz sampling rate. Paired images
were presented using E-Prime software (v1.4; Psychology Software
Tools, Pittsburgh, PA, United States). The participants were seated
approximately 70 cm from a 19” monitor with a 1920 × 1080 pixel
resolution. Before each session, TX300’s built-in calibration routine
was performed for each participant. The raw eye-tracking data files
were imported into SAS/STAT software (Version 9.4; SAS Institute
Inc., Cary, NC, USA) for processing.

prereg_statement_verbatim: The study protocol received approval from the Stanford University Institutional Review Board and was pre-registered with clinicaltrials.gov (NCT05864235) with original names of the interventions being: Control, Goal Support, and Goal Support + Coaching. For this pilot study, we renamed Goal Support + Coaching to ASPIRE. Two other modifications were made to the registered trial. Firstly, due to concerns regarding the legal implications of underage drinking and the absence of a Certificate of Confidentiality, enrollment was restricted to individuals aged 21 years and older. Secondly, a programming error that failed to notify Goal Support participants about their web-based follow-ups led us to analyze outcomes at 6- and 12-weeks solely in the Control and ASPIRE groups.

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "The service user evaluation form developed by the team to gather qualitative and quantitative data about service users’ experiences of the FTLBs psychoeducation group was completed by parents, young people, and professionals. Questions were based on previous questionnaires administered by the team to service users. The quantitative data consisted of asking service users their agreement with statements on a 5/6 point Likert scale to statements (the higher the score, the higher the level of agreement):
• The functional tics group was helpful’
• ‘I learnt a lot from the functional tics group’
• ‘I understand what functional tics are and how they can be managed’
• ‘All my questions were answered by the group’
• ‘I would recommend the functional tics group to other young people with functional tics and their parents’
Parents were asked to rate an additional statement:
• ‘I feel confident in supporting my child with their functional tics’
Professionals were asked to rate the following statements on a 4-point Likert scale (the higher the score, the more helpful they found the group):
• ‘How useful did you find the functional tics psychoeducation group you attended?’
• ‘Did this group cover the topics you thought should be included in a patient psychoeducation group?’
• ‘After attending the group, how confident do you feel offering treatment to a young person with functional tics?’
In addition to collecting quantitative data, open ended questions were asked to parents, young people, and professionals. Open ended questions included:
• ‘Please tell us what you enjoyed about the functional tics psychoeducation group’
• ‘Please tell us what you would have improved about the functional tics psychoeducation group’
• ‘What are your thoughts about doing the functional tics psychoeducation group by video?’
• ‘Is there anything else you would like to tell us about the service you received?’
Young people were asked an additional question:
• ‘What does your diagnosis mean to you?’
Professionals were asked:
• ‘Was there anything that you found useful about joining this group?’
• ‘What could we improve?’
• ‘Do you have any further comments?’
Feedback was gathered via telephone, or via the patient messaging system where necessary."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "The measures used are described below and in Table S1."


2. "Confirmed COVID-19, coded ‘1’, is confirmed by any test; unconfirmed COVID-19, coded ‘0’, is suspected by participant or doctor but not tested. Long COVID composite is a composite of physical symptoms reported at 4–20 weeks after onset of COVID-19 infection, and psychological symptoms. Ongoing COVID-19: symptoms 4 weeks or more after the onset of the original COVID-19 infection. Post-COVID-19 syndrome: symptoms 12 weeks or more after the onset of the original COVID-19 infection. Depression symptoms measured using the Short Mood and Feelings Questionnaire (SMFQ), anxiety symptoms measured using the Generalised Anxiety Disorder-7 questionnaire (GAD-7) and wellbeing measured using the Warwick-Edinburgh Mental Well-being Scale (WEMWS). T-tests were used to assess differences between groups for continuous outcomes, and chi-squared tests for dichotomous outcomes."

3. "At each questionnaire, participants self-reported having had COVID-19 with four response options: ‘yes, confirmed by a positive test’, ‘yes, suspected by a doctor but not tested’, ‘yes, my own suspicions’ or ‘no’. The primary exposure variable was ‘confirmation of COVID-19 infection status’, for which ‘yes, confirmed by a positive test’ responses were classified as ‘confirmed COVID-19’, and ‘yes, suspected by a doctor but not tested’ or ‘yes, my own suspicions’ responses were classified as ‘unconfirmed COVID-19’. The secondary exposure variable was ‘COVID-19 infection status’ (no vs yes). ‘Yes, confirmed by a positive test’, ‘yes, suspected by a doctor but not tested’ and ‘yes, my own suspicions’ were classified as ‘yes’, and ‘no’ responses as ‘no’."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "The independent variable ‘being an AFM’ was measured with three questions: ‘Is there anyone in your family with behavioral and/or health problems due to the consumption of (1) alcohol / (2) illegal drugs / (3) painkillers and sedatives?’ Ten (multiple) answer categories were presented: (1) father; (2) mother; (3) brother; (4) sister; (5) partner; (6) stepfather; (7) stepmother; (8) stepbrother/stepsister; (9) son/daughter; (10) someone else. Answers were categorized as 0 = no, 1 = yes. If the participant identified one or more relatives with problematic substance use, the participant was considered an AFM. To test whether the number of relatives with problematic substance use related to the outcomes of the study, a variable was constructed (1 = one; 2 = two or more). In addition, we distinguished between genetic relatives (parents, siblings) and non-genetic relatives (stepparents, stepsiblings, partners) (1 = genetic; 2 = non-genetic)."


2. "Health was assessed by five items of the Short Form Health Survey-12 (SF-12) (Ware et al., 1996). The SF-12 is designed to measure health for physical and mental domains. Translation of the SF-36 into Dutch followed the stepwise, iterative procedures developed by the IQOLA Project (Aaronson et al., 1998). The SF-12 has good psychometric properties (Gandek et al., 1998). We used five items about general physical and mental health: (a) ‘In general, would you say your physical health is…?’ and (b) ‘In general, would you say your mental health is…?’ The five answer options were (1) poor, (2) fair, (3) good, (4) very good, (5) excellent. (c) ‘During the past four weeks, how often have you felt calm and peaceful?’, (d) ‘…how often did you have a lot of energy?’ and (e) ‘…how often have you felt downhearted and blue?’ There were six answer options: (1) never, (2) rarely, (3) sometimes, (4) often, (5) mostly, (6) always."

3. "The context of drinking was assessed with the questions: (a) ‘How often in the past 12 months did you drink alone?’ and (b) ‘How often in the past 12 months did you drink with friends?’ These questions had five response categories: (1) never/almost never, (2) sometimes, (3) half of the time, (4) most of the time, (5) almost always/always (Van Damme et al., 2018). Only alcohol users in past month were included in the data analysis.

Alcohol and/or drug use at the university was measured with five questions from a questionnaire of the city of Rotterdam (City of Rotterdam, 2017) (0 = no; 1 = yes). ‘Have you during the past 4 weeks at university: (a) …been under the influence of alcohol?’, (b) ‘…been under the influence of drugs?’, (c) ‘…had a hangover (from alcohol, soft drugs or hard drugs)?’. ‘Have you, during the past 4 weeks at university, during a break or in a free period of time, (a) …used alcohol?’, (b) ‘…used drugs?’.

One self-designed question for AFMs was added: ‘If a relative drinks too much/uses drugs in a problematic way, do you feel safe enough to discuss this with someone at university, such as a teacher or study coach?’ Three answers were possible: (1) yes, (2) no, (3) I do feel safe to do so, but I am too ashamed to do it."

4. "The negative consequences of alcohol and substance use were assessed by three items selected from the Core Alcohol and Drug Survey (CADS), short form (CORE Institute, 2015): ‘How often have you experienced the following due to your drinking or drug use?’ (a) I performed poorly on a test or important project, (b) I missed class and (c) I suffered from memory loss. There were six response categories: (1) never; (2) once; (3) twice; (4) three to five times; (5) six to nine times; (6) ten times or more. We used the Dutch translation of the CADS that was prepared for the Flemish inter-university project ‘Head in the clouds’, aimed at measuring substance use among students (Van Damme et al., 2018)."

5. "We determined study success on the basis of the study year item. Respondents who indicated they were in the second, third or fourth study year with delay or had been studying for five or more years (which is the official cutoff point for study delay in a bachelor program in the Netherlands) were coded 0, while students with no delay were coded 1 on the variable ‘study success’. Because the study took place in the months of December and January, first-year students could by definition not be considered as possibly delayed. Delay can only be established at the end of the first study year, in July. Therefore, first year students were excluded from this parameter"

analysis_statement: YES — the statement says that the analysis scripts are available
analysis_statement_verbatim:
"I adopted Braun and Clarke’s (Citation2020) 6-phase method of reflexive thematic analysis, however, it is important to state that this was not a linear sequential approach, rather the development of themes was an organic process which involved going back and forward amongst the phases over a period of several weeks (see Fryer, Citation2022).

Phase 1 – familiarisation with the data. This was an ongoing process, beginning with the interviews themselves, continuing through the process of listening back and correcting the transcriptions for errors and true punctuation, and then immersion through repeated reading of the data when I started to log early observations about coding and patterning.

Phase 2 – generation of initial codes. At this stage I chose to code as broadly and descriptively as possible, even if this meant that some codes lacked compatibility. Once this process was complete, I returned to the dataset several times to evaluate the preliminary coding. My intention had been to begin to generate early thematic labels but, on further reflection, I realised that these were actually domain summaries or topics which, while still useful for grouping the codes, were insufficient in capturing rich, multi-faceted meanings within the data (Braun & Clarke, Citation2020).

Phase 3 – searching for themes. I began to map the codes and domain summaries as a way of visualising the data, playing with early thematic ideas and labels by moving the codes around the map. The map went through several rounds of iterations and consolidation to reach a set of 5 candidate themes and a series of associated potential sub themes (Appendix B).

Phase 4 – reviewing themes. When reviewing my candidate themes, I frequently returned to the interview data for two reasons. Firstly, it was important that I could re-contextualise a theme within participants’ original accounts. Secondly, and related to this, I began to seek narrative extracts that I could use to bring the theme to life within my final report.

The process of refining the candidate themes continued. Some themes were collapsed into one to create a more parsimonious structure, for example ‘safety in mess-making’, and ‘therapist as container’ were subsumed within the broader theme of ‘the active therapist’. Through this process, 4 distinct core themes with associated subthemes were established and given preliminary labels.

Phase 5 – defining and naming themes. The 4 final themes were assigned an accompanying narrative which distilled the theme and contained a synopsis of key subthemes. Each theme was given a name designed to create further distinction between them before sharing them with opted-in participants. Three participants responded and concurred with the analysis.

Phase 6 – producing the report. The final stage of the analysis involved reviewing participant feedback on the themes for discrepancies, adding context to the data story and evidencing the themes through data extracts, and assembling it into a single holistic narrative."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "We asked participants to indicate what kind of sex toys they owned. Participants were presented with 11 categories of sex toys, namely phallus-shaped vibrator, clit sucker, dildo, BDSM equipment, objects for the penis (e.g., ring), lingerie, plug, vaginal or anal beads, wand, and strap-on. These categories were chosen bearing in mind to (1) encompass most of non-digital, non-virtual, and non-personified devices, (2) consider both insertable and non-insertable devices and (3) consider devices that stimulate parts of the body that are either congruent and incongruent with the traditional vision of typical sexual organs (i.e., penis and vagina). NOte that the clit sucker has rarely been included in previous research as a separate sex toy and presumably, included in the vibrator category. We decided to consider the clit sucker as a separate category because it is based primarily on suction rather than vibration, and because it is designed to not be inserted. For each of these categories, participants could indicate “yes” or “no” depending on whether they had or did not have these kinds of sex toys. Participants could select one or all of the categories so that this measure ranged from 1 to 11."

2. "The variables assessed were the participants’ sex assigned at birth (“What is the sex assigned to you at birth?” with male, female, and intersexual options), gender identity (“Which of these best reflects your gender identity?” with male, female, transgender, non-binary, and other options), sexual orientation (“Which of the following definitions best describes your sexual orientation?” with straight, bisexual, lesbian, gay, asexual, pansexual, queer, not defined, and other options), age (“Please, indicate your age in numbers”) and level of education (“What is the highest level of education you have achieved?” with a high school diploma or lower degree, bachelor's degree, master's degree, postgraduate master, and Ph.D. options)."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. Three-item Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test (Bradley et al., 2007), which is a brief valid screening instrument widely used for heavy drinking and/or probable current AUD, with test–retest reliability of κ ranging from 0.62 to 0.78 (Bell & Britton, 2015). The Alcohol Use Disorders Identification Test items inquire about the frequency of drinking, the number of drinks on a typical drinking day, and heavy drinking defined as six or more drinks on one occasion. Participants provided responses on a scale of 0–4, and a sum of 3+ for men and 4+ for women was established as indicating probable AUD (Bradley et al., 2007). Transgender, gender nonconforming, or “other” (noncisgender) participants were categorized using the 4+ criteria, with the 3+ criteria also utilized to estimate alternative probable AUD prevalence."

2. "Probable DUD. The two-item Screen of Drug Use (SoDU; Tiet et al., 2015) is a validated and practical screening tool for identifying current, probable DUDs and drug use with serious consequences related to illicit drugs and nonprescribed prescription medications. It was designed for use in primary care settings for adults. The SoDU comprises two items, the first of which asks participants how many days in the past 12 months they have “used drugs other than alcohol” (with a response of ≥7 days indicating probable DUD). If not, the second item asks how many days they have “used drugs more than you meant to” (with a response of ≥2 days indicating probable DUD). The SoDU has demonstrated sensitivity and specificity of over 90% for detecting DUDs and drug use with serious consequences and test–retest reliability of κ = 0.90 (Tiet et al., 2015)"

3. General and Financial Stress. For general stress, participants
were asked “What is your current stress level, rating from 0 (not at
all) to 10 (highest level imaginable)?” This measure was created for
this study and has been shown to be highly correlated with quality of
life, depression and anxiety symptoms, and physical pain levels
(Tiet et al., 2024). Similarly, participants were asked to rate their
current level of financial stress between 0 and 10. Higher numbers
represent “greater financial stress” for their financial stress. This
measure has also been shown to be associated with mental health
and physical pain outcomes (Tiet et al., 2024)."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "Parental identity Salience
A single-item Parental Identity Salience Scale was adapted from the Quinn and Chaudoir’s Identity Salience Scale [22]. The item states, “How often do you generally think about your parental identity?” Participants responded using a 7-point scale from (1) almost never to (7) many times each day."

2. "Parental Intention to Vaccinate
Participants rated on a 7-point scale, from (1) extremely unlikely to (7) extremely likely, to indicate their likelihood to (1) take the COVID-19 vaccine, (2) vaccinate their children (over 6-month-old) against COVID-19, and seek information about the COVID-19 vaccine from their doctor/pediatrician. Given that the mass vaccination program for individuals over 18 started before data collection, participants were given an opportunity to indicate their vaccination status instead of answering the question pertaining to parent-vaccination intention. If participants indicated that they were vaccinated before the experiment, their responses in this item were treated as missing data."

coi_statement_verbatim: The first author is a literary scholar with several years of experience in the analysis of personal documents such as travel accounts, letters, memoirs, and diaries, as well as first-person narratives in fictional prose. Her approach to such writings refers to the understanding of the autobiographical texts presented by Smith and Watson (Citation2010), specifying their research subject as “life writing as a general term for writing that takes a life, one’s own or another’s, as its subject. Such writing can be biographical, novelistic, historical, or explicitly self-referential and therefore autobiographical” (p. 4). Hence, the fact that there were personal narratives that she analyzed, not literary ones, did not limit the study in any way. She specialized in the writings of the Romantic period and examined the manners in which the tradition of this epoch both draws from and continues previous paradigms as well, is reanimated in the contemporary Polish culture. Her knowledge of the process of changing cultural patterns helps to clarify the outlines of the life stories. She participated in the research by the second author for 4 years and is well-acquainted with the methods and approaches of the field of psychology. The second author is a psychologist, with numerous years of experience researching narrative psychology and personality. He specializes in qualitative and mixed methodology, with extensive experience in analyzing life story interviews. His research interests include relations between narratives and one’s understanding of the purpose and meaning of life. He is also interested in posttraumatic growth and the factors that enable people to make positive changes in life after experiencing trauma. His recent research focused on identifying characteristics of narratives constructed after an experience of trauma that influences posttraumatic growth. His experience was of value for the interpretation of transformative elements in the life stories and understanding the Polish master narrative in the context of the redemption sequence. Both authors did their utmost to prevent a situation in which the master narratives are identified only by the analysts’ own interpretation and focused on the property of their content.

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "The primary outcome variable was restricted to negative urinalysis results for MA at 12 months after MTP treatment and at 36 months after MTP treatment. These variables were constructed from urinalysis result reports. The cut-off value for a positive MA result was 300 ng/ml."

2. "Administrative data used for these measures were taken from the MTP Treatment Tracking Form filled out by providers (see CitationHuber et al. 2000)."

3. "Monitoring drug use during treatment was operationalized as the client providing three consecutive MA-negative urinalyses (1 = yes, 0 = no), with “yes” referring to urinalysis results that were negative for MA use on three consecutive visits (weeks) during treatment."

4. "Two measures of drug use were also included in each model because of their likely relationship with a client's chances of relapse. The number of alcohol or drug treatments between the MTP treatment and follow-up consisted of the sums of two ASI items indicating the number of treatments for alcohol and for other drugs that a respondent had since the previous ASI was administered. The measure from baseline to the 12-month follow-up was added to the measure from the 12- to 36-month follow-up in order to create the variable that was included in the model predicting the 36-month MA urinalysis outcome. Additionally, analytic models included a baseline ASI measure indicating the number of days in the past 30 that a respondent had used MA."

measures_statement_verbatim:
"Below are the questions that we use to test the predictive power of our argument about the role of social trust contributing to polarization in the United States. We also test a set of alternative hypotheses to determine relative predictive power of the most common arguments about what drives said polarization.

We include a very common measure of relative social trust in the model to test our primary hypothesis. The question asks, Can people be trusted? The respondent has the choice of answering that either you can’t be too careful or most people can be trusted. [...]

[...] we include two religious measures (Abramowitz & Saunders, 2006; Carmines & Stanley, 1992; Franklin & Jackson, 1983). First, the variable examining religiosity asks, Would you say you religion provides some guidance in your day-to-day living, quite a bit of guidance, or a great deal of guidance in your day-to-day life? Respondents are coded for not believing religion is important, religion provides some guidance, religion provides quite a bit of guidance, and religion provides a great deal of guidance. The religious beliefs measure asks, Which of the these statements comes closest to describing your feelings about the Bible? Respondents are asked to choose from the following: 1 = The Bible is the actual word of God and is to be taken literally, word for word; 2 = The Bible is the word of God but not everything in it should be taken literally, word for word; and 3 = The Bible is a book written by men and is not the word of God.

We use a 7-point scale of ideology to measure where respondents place themselves in terms of political beliefs (Abramowitz & Saunders, 1998, 2006; Alvarez & Bedolla, 2003; Carmines & Berkman, 1994; Carmines & Stanley, 1992; Franklin & Jackson, 1983). The scale runs from 1, indicating strong liberal, to 7, indicating strong conservative. Strong liberals are predicted to be strong Democrats, and strong conservatives are most likely to be strong Republicans. [...] 

We also include a number of control variables for gender, age, education, and income as these are very common controls in studies of public attitudes toward polarization in the United States. We also include a measure of job security as a way to control for the economic insecurity of the respondent. We include this measure because it is possible that the respondent’s economic insecurity can affect the responses given in the survey."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "2.2.1. Parental smoking
Parental smoking was assessed with two questions “Does your mother/father smoke?” Response options were: 1 (my mother/father has never smoked), 2 (my mother/father quit smoking), 3 (my mother/father smokes), 4 (I don't have a mother/father), and 5 (I do not know). Scores for mothers and fathers were dichotomized (0 = not currently smoking, 1 = currently smoking) and summed, to indicate the number of smoking parents." 

2. "To assess sibling smoking, children were asked to report the names of all siblings and for each sibling indicate whether he/she smokes. Response options were: 0 (no, he/she doesn't smoke) and 1 (yes, he/she smokes). Scores of all siblings were summed, to indicate the number of smoking siblings."

3. "To assess the number of smoking peers, children were asked to report the number of friends who smoke. Response options were: 0 (nobody), 1 (1 friend), 2 (2 friends), 3 (3 friends), 4 (4 friends), and 5 (5 or more friends)."

4. "To assess perceived safety of casual smoking, children were asked to indicate the degree to which they agree with three statements on a scale ranging from 1 (totally disagree) to 4 (totally agree). The following items were used: “There is no harm in smoking a cigarette once in a while”, “It is safe to smoke for only one or two years”, and “If you only smoke once in a while you won't become addicted” (see Siegel, Alvaro, & Burgoon, 2003). A mean score was calculated. Internal consistency was acceptable (Cronbach's alpha = .63)."

5. "To assess susceptibility to smoking, children were asked to select the statement that best describes them. Response options were: 1 (I know for sure that I will never start smoking), 2 (I think that I will never start smoking), 3 (I think I will try smoking in the future), 4 (I think I will try smoking within five years), 5 (I think I will try smoking within one year), 6 (I think I will try smoking within six months), 7 (I think I will try smoking within one month), and 8 (I have already tried smoking). Due to a preponderance of scores at the scale minimum, the scores were dichotomized into 0 (I know for sure that I will never start smoking) and 1 (any other response), thereby indicating (the lack of) a resolute intention to refrain from smoking (Pierce et al., 1996)."

measures_statement_verbatim: 1. "Measures of the economic aspect of SES. There is currently very little consensus on the best ways to measure SES, but we were focused particularly on the economic aspect of SES (Adler et al., 1994). We used three different measures: income, subjective fi- nancial strain, and relative status. Level of education was con- trolled in all analyses (Adler et al., 1994). To assess household income we asked, “How much was your total family income from all sources last year before taxes and other deductions?” If participants would not give an exact number, they were asked a follow-up question to determine which income range they fell into (e.g., $15,000 –$19,999, etc.). To create a continuous measure of income, participants who selected an in- come range were assigned the midpoint of that range as their income. The total mean household income was $61,299.36 (SD $49,250.71). The median income was $50,000. Finally, we divided the household income by the number of adults and children living in the household. This allowed us to use a more accurate estimate of the person’s level of financial resources controlling for house- hold size, and this is consistent with federal guidelines for identi- fying families in poverty (U.S. Department of Health & Human Services, 2009). The mean individual income (in 2003 dollars) was $25,542.51 (SD  $27,331). The subjective appraisal of financial strain was assessed with the question: “After paying all of the household bills at the end of the month, does your family usually have. . . 1) More than enough money left over; 2) Some money left over; 3) Just enough to make ends meet, or 4) NOt enough to make ends meet.” The mean was 2.2 (SD  .89)."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "Involvement in bullying. The survey defined bullying as "when a person or group of people repeatedly say or do mean or hurtful things to someone on purpose. It typically occurs when there is a power imbalance" (Nansel et al., 2001; Olweus, 1993). The survey also provided examples of bullying in "teasing, hitting, threatening, name-calling, ignoring, stealing from, cyberbullying, spreading rumors, and leaving someone out on purpose" (see Bradshaw et al., 2007). Victimization was assessed through a single item, "How frequently was your child bullied at school in the past month?" to which parents chose from the frequency options of several times a week, once a week, two to three times during the past month, one time during the past month, or my child has not been bullied in the past month. Perpetration of bullying was assessed through the question, "How frequently did your child bully others at school in the past month?" which used the same response options. Children were placed into victim or perpetrator categories (or both) if they had been involved as either "in the past month"-a cutoff time point used in previous ASD-focused bullying studies (Cappadocia, Weiss, & Pepler, 2012)."

2. "School climate. Perceptions of the school climate were assessed through the following 10 statements, for which parents rated their level of agreement on a 4-point Likert scale (strongly disagree, disagree, agree, strongly agree): (a) The teachers care about my child; (b) My child enjoys school; (c) My child feels safe at school; (d) The school is an orderly environment for learning; (e) My child feels like he or she belongs at his or her school; (f) I feel that the adults at my child's school are doing enough to prevent or stop bullying; (g) Bullying is a serious problem at my child's school; (h) If I need to talk about my child's progress or problems, I am able to contact his or her teacher(s); (i) I have a good relationship with my child's teacher(s); and (j) My child's school encourages parent involvement. This measure was adapted from a previous scale used by Waasdorp et al. (2011)."

3. "Parental school involvement. Parental school involvement required parentsto indicate the frequency (never, rarely, sometimes, often, very often, almost always) with which they participate in school related activities. The four statements inquired about the frequency of (a) attending parent-teacher conferences; (b) attending school functions such as open houses, book fairs, and PTA meetings; (c) volunteering at the child's school; and (d) being involved in the child's schoolwork and/or homework."

measures_statement_verbatim:
1. "Evaluation of Appearance
Rating of physical appearance was quantified using the Appearance Rating Scale, an 11-point numeric rating scale with higher values indicating a higher perceived impairment in appearance (Stangier, Hungerbuhler, Meyer, & Wolter, 2000). The question posed to patients was, “To what degree do you feel that your physical appearance is impaired?” The question given to raters was changed for the present study into, “To what degree do you feel this person's physical appearance is impaired?” Possible answers ranged from 0 (= not at all impaired) to 10 (= most severely impaired). As patients’ instruction did not include the body part that had to be rated, they were additionally asked to indicate the part of their body their individual rating referred to."

coi_statement_verbatim:
"Therapist
The therapist (also the second author) was male, older than the client, European-American and highly experienced as a Process-Experiential therapist (he is one of the originators of the approach).

Researchers
In contrast to previous CPA analyses (Hardy, Rees, Barkham, et al., 1998; Rees, Hardy, Barkham, et al., 2001) a team of judges was not used to consensualise the findings. Instead the researcher carried out the analysis, which was then carefully audited by the research supervisor (the second author and therapist).

The first author has a person-centred approach toward therapy; she believes that when clients are offered a non-judgemental relationship of empathy and acceptance they may choose to reveal previously unspoken thoughts and feelings. She views disclosing in therapy as a complex decision for clients, which is often the first step on the way to change. Carrying out this research, she found listening to the recordings of the client's sessions a very rich experience; she felt very warmly towards the client, especially the client's openness about the traumatic events she had undergone.

The second author has a humanistic, person-centred-experiential theoretical orientation toward psychotherapy in general, which includes a key value placed on acceptance of all aspects of the client; in particular, he sees client disclosure of previously hidden secrets or shameful personal information as playing an important role in the change process, analogous to the role of confession in traditional spiritual traditions, as long as this is handled non-coercively, carefully and with respect. He was also able to draw on his memories as therapist in auditing the analysis of this event."

analysis_statement_verbatim: "Content analysis was performed in three phases. Phase one involved the three members of the research team repeatedly reading the transcripts independently, aiming to achieve in-depth acquaintance with the text (Kvale 1996). In each reading, the authors were involved in a dialogue between their professional and research experiences and their reading of the text of transcribed interviews. Phase two was performed independently also, in which each author developed his initial codes and generated a thematic coding framework for analyzing the interviews. More specifically, these were performed by a) Open coding: (Strauss and Corbin 1998). In this step, each author read the interview and coded the data line by line. NOtes were made throughout the text to identify initial units of meaning (categories) that emerged from the data (e.g., “negative emotion in the relationships with batterers;” “perceived positive impacts of working with batterers”). b) Axial coding: (Strauss and Corbin 1998). In this second step, relationships between categories previously identified were recognized, contextualized and organized (e.g., “parallels between one’s own attitudes toward masculine identity and the professional relationship”). The three researchers compiled a joint list of units of meaning from their separate lists, and reached approximately 85% agreement regarding the categories that emerged from the data. Disagreement and discrepancies in coding and the developing conception were resolved through discussion, as well as joint review of and reflection on the original statements. In the few cases where this could not be achieved, the units were eliminated from the study (e.g. the PV work has mostly negative influence). This phase ended when the organization of the different units of meaning that the interviewees attached to their work experience reached saturation."

measures_statement_verbatim:
"Behavioral intention to adhere to the “five-a-day” recommendation, behavioral intention was measured by a single item: “I intend to eat at least five portions of fruit and vegetables a day” (5-point scale ranging from strongly disagree to strongly agree).

Behavioral expectation for eating “five-a-day” was measured using a single item: “I am going to eat at least five portions of fruit and vegetables a day” (5-point scale ranging from strongly disagree to strongly agree).

Behavior
Participants reported how many servings of (a) vegetables (excluding potatoes), and (b) fruit (fresh, frozen, or canned) they had eaten in the past week at baseline and at 1 week follow-up. The response options for both of these measures were “less than 1 per week,” “1 per week,” “2–4 per week,” “5–6 per week,” “1 per day,” “2 per day,” “3 per day,” “4 per day,” or “5 or more per day.” These responses were converted to an average number of portions of fruit and/or vegetables consumed per day. Average consumption per day was then converted into a binary variable for whether a participant had consumed five or more servings of fruit and/or vegetables a day."

measures_statement_verbatim: "To measure sexual satisfaction, we asked, “How satisfied are you with your sex life at this moment?” derived from The Sexuality Scale (Snell & Papini, 1989). Participants responded on a 5-point scale ranging from 1 (very unsatisfied) to 5 (very satisfied). 68 H. Kedde et al. Participants received a self-constructed question about the importance of sexuality in their lives, which we measured also on a 5-point scale ranging from 1 (least important) to 5 (very important). We measured the frequency of talking about sexuality and intimacy on a 5-point scale ranging from 1 (never) to 5 (very often). The following question was with whom they talked about sexuality and intimacy. Response options were the following: partner, friend/acquaintance, family member, general practitioner, and other professional health care worker (open input). In addition, we assessed problems directly related to the respondent’s impairment concerning sexuality. Questions were phrased as followed: “Due to my disablility . . . ” “ . . . I experience problems in my sexual relationship(s)” ‘ . . . I experience practical sexual problems” “ . . . is sexuality not part of my life” “ . . . is it difficult to find a sexual partner” “ . . . I cannot enjoy my sexuality” “ . . . I experience my sexual life as problematic”"

measures_statement_verbatim:
"Physical activity. Physical activity was measured as total
step counts per day by the ActiGraph Model 7164 accelerometer.
The ActiGraph Model 7164 accelerometer contains a single, vertical axis piezoelectric bender element that generates an electrical
signal proportional to the force acting on it. The acceleration/
deceleration signal is digitized by an analog-to-digital converter
and numerically integrated over a preprogrammed epoch interval.
After each interval ends, the integrated value of step counts per
epoch is stored in random access memory and the integrator is
reset. The monitor is programmed for start time and data collection
interval and data are retrieved for analysis via a personal computer
interface and software provided with the unit. The data that are
downloaded from the accelerometers are then entered into Microsoft Excel for data processing. The epoch was 1 minute in this
study, and the accelerometers were worn on an elastic belt around
the waist on the nondominant hip during the waking hours, except
while showering, bathing, and swimming, for a 7-day period.
Waking hours was defined as the moment upon getting out of bed
in the morning through the moment of getting into bed in the
evening. The participants recorded the time that the accelerometer
was worn on a log, and this was verified by inspection of the
minute-by-minute accelerometer data. Regarding data processing,
we summed the minute-by-minute step counts across each of the 7
days and then averaged the total step counts across the 7 days.Evidence for the reliability and validity of scores from this accelerometer has been provided for persons with MS (Motl, McAuley,
Snook, & Scott, 2006; Motl et al., 2007)"
